items.title: Fair die expected value and variance
items.scenario: A six-sided fair die has X ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} with equal probability 1/6 each.
items.explanation: E[X] = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 3.5. E[X²] = (1+4+9+16+25+36)/6 = 91/6, so Var(X) = 91/6 − 12.25 = 35/12 ≈ 2.92. The mean is the center of the symmetric discrete distribution; variance quantifies typical spread (~1.71 faces).

items.title: API latency SLA planning
items.scenario: Production logs show mean API latency μ = 120 ms with standard deviation σ = 40 ms. Leadership asks whether p95 latency is likely under 200 ms.
items.explanation: E[X] and σ summarize the latency distribution. With roughly symmetric data, μ + 2σ ≈ 200 ms is a quick heuristic for tail risk; exact p95 needs the full CDF or empirical quantile. Expectation sets the typical case; variance (via σ) indicates how often large delays occur.

items.title: Linearity for expected cost of A/B test traffic
items.scenario: An experiment sends fraction p of users to variant A (expected revenue $3/user) and fraction (1−p) to variant B (expected revenue $4/user). Total expected revenue per user is needed without assuming independence between assignment and user type.
items.explanation: Let I_A be the indicator that a user sees A. E[revenue] = E[3·I_A + 4·(1−I_A)] = 3·E[I_A] + 4·(1 − E[I_A]) = 3p + 4(1−p) = 4 − p. Linearity holds because expectation splits over the sum—even though assignment and user value may be correlated within each arm.

items.title: MSE loss and predicting the mean
items.scenario: A regression model predicts house prices. Training minimizes mean squared error (MSE) between prediction ŷ and true price y.
items.explanation: For a fixed feature vector x, the value of ŷ that minimizes E[(y − ŷ)² | x] is ŷ = E[y | x]—the conditional mean. MSE loss therefore pushes the model toward expected targets under squared-error optimality. RMSE is √(average squared error), linking evaluation directly to variance-like quantities.
